findings.description: clamp_to_range does not guard datetime awareness (unlike humanize_delta), so passing a mix of naive and aware datetimes among value/lower/upper raises an undocumented TypeError from the < / min / max comparisons instead of the documented ValueError, making behavior inconsistent across the module's two datetime-comparing functions.

clamp_to_range uses strict 'upper < lower' so an inverted-by-equality is allowed (upper == lower yields a single-point range), which is defensible, but the comparison itself can raise TypeError on tz-mixed inputs because there is no awareness precondition; the only validation performed is the ordering check.

The awareness guard checks only 'tzinfo is None'; a datetime carrying a non-None tzinfo whose utcoffset() returns None (permitted by the tzinfo ABC) passes the guard yet still makes the subtraction at line 56 raise TypeError, so the guarantee 'both datetimes must be timezone-aware' is not fully enforced.

parse_iso_date is documented as parsing 'strictly' YYYY-MM-DD, but CPython's strptime with %Y/%m/%d accepts non-zero-padded fields (e.g. '2024-3-5') and leading-whitespace variants, so the strictness promise is weaker than the docstring implies.

humanize_delta truncates fractional seconds via int(total_seconds()) before the <0 check, so a moment fractionally in the future (e.g. 0.4s ahead) yields seconds==0 and is reported as 'just now' rather than 'in the future'; the future branch only triggers at a full second or more ahead.

day_range eagerly materializes one date object per day, so a large span (and business_days_between which delegates to it) allocates a list proportional to the interval length with no upper bound — a memory/perf concern noted in passing; defer detail to critic-performance.
